reasoning:
The 90-day average of SOFR is a backward-looking 90-calendar-day trailing average of spot SOFR. Spot SOFR from 90 days prior to mid-April (i.e. mid-January) was generally around 4.30-4.33, while recent spot SOFR in April has been printed higher, typically 4.35-4.42 (including a quarter-end spike). Because higher current values are replacing lower historical values in the rolling window, the 90-day average has been monotonically increasing since early April (reaching 4.3588 by April 11). 
For the April 20 horizon, there are no Fed meetings, so this upward drift is highly likely to continue. 
For May 13, the May FOMC meeting introduces a potential rate cut; a 25 bps cut would sharply pull spot SOFR down for 6 days prior to the May 13 resolution, but it first has to overcome ~24 days of upward drift. I assess this as still favoring an increase (P=0.63).
By July 12, the market widely anticipates the Fed will have delivered at least one if not two rate cuts, accumulating enough low spot days to drag the 90-day average below the April 13 baseline (P=0.39).
For October 10, the macroeconomic environment (inflation reacceleration vs recession) introduces extreme uncertainty. Following the instruction to increase uncertainty and keep distant forecasts near 0.5, I have drawn the probability closest to 50% for this longest horizon (P=0.46).
